witnesses.description: The powerset lattice of {a,b}, encoded as four finite elements.
The monotone map that adds A to each element of the Boolean lattice.

checks.claim: The listed subset relation is a partial order on the four Boolean-lattice elements.
checks.notes: The validator checks reflexivity, antisymmetry, transitivity, and the listed top/bottom elements.

checks.claim: The listed meet and join tables are greatest lower bound and least upper bound operations for the finite order.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes every finite lower and upper bound set and checks the listed meet/join entries.

checks.claim: The listed finite lattice is distributive.
checks.notes: The validator checks x meet (y join z) = (x meet y) join (x meet z) and the dual law for all triples.

checks.claim: The map that adds A is monotone and has the listed fixed points with A least.
checks.notes: The validator checks monotonicity over every comparable pair, recomputes fixed points, and checks the listed least fixed point.

checks.claim: The listed relation is a partial order.
checks.notes: The validator checks the relation is reflexive and transitive but violates antisymmetry because x <= y and y <= x while x != y.

checks.claim: General order, lattice, and fixed-point theory requires future proof resources.

data.target_theorem_shape: complete lattice fixed-point theorems, order-theoretic induction, domain theory, Galois connections, abstract interpretation, distributive/Boolean algebra representation, and infinite posets
data.future_checker: Lean modules for order theory, lattice theory, complete lattices, fixed-point theorems, Galois connections, and proof reconstruction from finite relation certificates

checks.notes: The finite rows are checked by exact table replay; general lattice and fixed-point theory remains proof-assistant territory.
